did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a novel Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model named SkinCNN for the classification of seven types of skin cancer lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc). The study utilized deep learning techniques, specifically modifying the MobileNet V3 architecture with a swish activation function and custom convolutional layers, to achieve a 97% accuracy rate in skin cancer classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where a novel CNN model (SkinCNN) was developed and evaluated for classifying skin cancer lesions. It details the methodology (modifying MobileNet V3, using a specific dataset of 10,015 images, 5-fold cross-validation), reports specific performance metrics (97% accuracy, F1 score 0.98), and compares these results against baseline models and other studies, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: SkinCNN: Classification of Skin Cancer Lesions with A Novel CNN Model
journal: Bitlis Eren Üniversitesi Fen Bilimleri Dergisi
brief_summary: This study developed SkinCNN, a novel lightweight Convolutional Neural Network model for classifying seven types of skin cancer lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) using a dataset of 10,015 labeled images from Austrian dermatology departments. The proposed model modifies the MobileNet V3 architecture by replacing the ReLU activation function with swish and introducing custom convolutional layers, eliminating the need for pre-trained weights. Evaluation was conducted using 5-fold cross-validation, comparing SkinCNN against the standard MobileNet V3 model. SkinCNN achieved superior performance with 97% accuracy, an F1 score of 0.98, and a recall of 0.97, significantly outperforming MobileNet V3 which scored 83% accuracy and an F1 score of 0.87. The results indicate that SkinCNN is faster, more accurate, and competitive with existing literature, making it suitable for real-time diagnostic support systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes examined in the study. The dataset contained 327 images for this class. It is noted as one of the most common types and potentially more lethal if not treated early.

all_task_types.target: Seven types of skin cancer lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops SkinCNN for the classification of seven discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer lesions. The model predicts class labels (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) using a dataset of 10,015 labeled images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (97%), F1 score (0.98), recall (0.97), and precision (0.96). Although reference [30] in the comparison table mentions 'Regression based classifier', the primary task of this study is explicitly defined as classification of skin cancer types.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, prepared by skin specialists in the Dermatology department of Austria (specifically 2 different dermatology departments).

use.training: Used for training the SkinCNN and MobileNet V3 models after data augmentation to equalize class counts.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To partition the dataset into training and test sets for model evaluation and to ensure the reliability of the model by obtaining similar results across different experimental runs.
To eliminate data imbalance between classes by applying horizontal and vertical rotation to images, thereby equalizing the number of images per class.
To train the models with a learning rate of 0.001, Beta 1 of 0.9, and Beta 2 of 0.999.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, F1 score, recall, precision) and training speed, without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: SkinCNN achieved superior performance compared to MobileNet V3. The study highlights that SkinCNN is faster, lighter, and does not require pre-trained weights. The accuracy difference between the two models is noted as approximately 15% in training and 20% in test accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: SkinCNN (a novel CNN model based on MobileNet V3 architecture)
architecture_used.pretraining: None; the model was built from scratch without using pre-trained weights, unlike the baseline MobileNet V3 which used transfer learning.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (dataset consisted of dermatoscopic images from HAM10000)

activation_functions: Swish (used instead of ReLU in the core structure)
architecture_details.custom_components: Core structure with swish activation; custom convolutional layers with 3x3 kernels and 16, 32, 64 filters; multiple dropout layers (0.2% and 0.3%); batch normalization layers.

training_details.data_augmentation: Horizontal and vertical rotation to equalize class counts

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via horizontal and vertical rotation of images to eliminate class imbalance and equalize image counts across classes.
Normalization of inputs between layers using Batch Normalization layers within the SkinCNN architecture.
Feature selection via Max Pooling layers with 2x2 window sizes.
Noise reduction in convolutional layers to extract discriminative features.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to images on a class basis to eliminate data imbalance. These operations were applied to all classes other than the majority class (nv/melanocytic nevi) to equalize the number of images per class with the nv class.

conclusions: The proposed SkinCNN model achieved a 97% accuracy rate, an F1 score of 0.98, a recall of 0.97, and a precision of 0.96 in classifying seven types of skin cancer lesions.
SkinCNN performed significantly better than the baseline MobileNet V3 model, which achieved an accuracy of 83% and an F1 score of 0.87.
The SkinCNN model completed the training process 30 times faster than the pre-trained MobileNet V3 model.
SkinCNN is a lightweight model built from scratch without using pre-trained weights, making it suitable for real-time diagnostic support systems.
The reliability of the SkinCNN model was ensured by obtaining consistent results across five-fold cross-validation experiments.
SkinCNN is competitive with existing studies in the literature, showing a performance difference of approximately 5% compared to other cited works.
The study demonstrated that CNN models without pre-trained weights can be effective for skin cancer classification when optimized with custom architectures and activation functions like swish.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 6,705 melanocytic nevi vs. 115 dermatofibroma) and demographic bias (predominantly Caucasian patients from Austrian dermatology departments), limiting generalizability to other populations.

Data augmentation (horizontal and vertical rotation) was applied specifically to equalize class counts with the majority class (nv), which may artificially inflate performance metrics for minority classes and does not reflect real-world prevalence.
The paper lacks external validation; results are derived solely from 5-fold cross-validation on a single dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and robustness in diverse clinical settings.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported performance differences between SkinCNN (97% accuracy) and MobileNet V3 (83% accuracy).

The comparison with existing literature in Table 3 is methodologically flawed due to heterogeneity in the number of classes (ranging from 1 to 10) and varying evaluation metrics, making direct performance comparisons unreliable.

The model was trained from scratch without pre-trained weights, which is a strength for lightweight deployment but may limit feature extraction capabilities compared to transfer learning approaches, especially given the limited size of the dataset for deep learning.

The paper does not report sensitivity/specificity trade-offs or confusion matrices, preventing assessment of class-specific errors, particularly for critical classes like melanoma (mel) and basal cell carcinoma (bcc).
The claim of 'real-time' applicability is based on training speed comparisons rather than inference latency benchmarks on target hardware, leaving deployment feasibility unverified.
